title: Save the Cat — 15-beat sheet
author: Blake Snyder
source: Save the Cat! The Last Book on Screenwriting You'll Ever Need, 2005.

lineage.note: The most prescriptive and most commercially dominant modern screenwriting framework. 15 beats with literal page numbers. Loved for usability, blamed for formulaic sameness. The B-story idea is worth stealing for any doc with parallel arcs.

stages.name: Opening Image
stages.function: First visual statement of the film's world and tone.

stages.name: Theme Stated
stages.function: Theme voiced (often indirectly) so later payoffs can resonate.

stages.name: Break into Two
stages.function: Commitment to Act II — the upside-down world.

stages.name: Fun and Games
stages.function: The premise delivered — 'the promise of the premise.'

stages.name: Midpoint
stages.function: False victory or false defeat; stakes raised.

stages.name: Dark Night of the Soul
stages.function: Wallowing in defeat; the protagonist must change to survive.

stages.name: Break into Three
stages.function: Insight from B-story plus A-story converge into a new plan.

stages.name: Final Image
stages.function: Mirror or contrast to the Opening Image — proof of change.

cross_framework_equivalence._note: Equivalence with most other modern frameworks is well-established; see field_paradigm, mckee_story, vogler_writers_journey, hauge_two_journeys for the canonical cross-table.

applicability.limitations: Famously prescriptive — applied rigidly, produces sameness. Treat the beats as places to ASK QUESTIONS, not slots to fill.
